title: Operation Rheinübung — The Bismarck Sortie
summary: Operation Rheinübung was the sortie of the battleship Bismarck and heavy cruiser Prinz Eugen into the Atlantic to attack Allied convoys. The operation ended with the sinking of HMS Hood and the destruction of the Bismarck nine days after she sailed.
context: Bismarck, the most powerful battleship in the world at the time of her commissioning, was intended as the centrepiece of German surface fleet commerce raiding strategy. Combined with the battlecruisers Scharnhorst and Gneisenau already at Brest, a successful Atlantic sortie could threaten Britain's supply lines in a way that U-boats alone could not. The operation's failure — and the loss of the Tirpitz's sister ship — convinced Hitler that risking capital ships in offensive operations was unacceptable. The German surface fleet thereafter played a largely defensive role. Fleet Commander Admiral Lütjens made a critical error by sending a long radio message on 25 May that allowed British forces to triangulate Bismarck's position after she had successfully broken contact.

key_events.event: Battle of the Denmark Strait — Bismarck sinks HMS Hood in under 10 minutes; 1,415 killed, 3 survivors. HMS Prince of Wales damaged.
Royal Navy hunts Bismarck across the Atlantic. Bismarck shakes pursuit but sends a long radio message that allows triangulation of her position.
Swordfish torpedo aircraft from HMS Ark Royal hit Bismarck's rudder, leaving her unable to manoeuvre.
HMS King George V and HMS Rodney shell Bismarck into a burning wreck. She sinks; 2,086 killed, 115 survivors.

outcome: Allied victory. Bismarck destroyed; Prinz Eugen escaped to Brest. The operation ended German hopes of disrupting Atlantic supply lines with surface raiders.

sources.ref: National Archives (UK). ADM 234/324: Battle Summary No. 5 — The Chase and Sinking of Bismarck.
Ballantyne, Iain. Killing the Bismarck. Pen and Sword, 2010.
Mullenheim-Rechberg, Burkard von. Battleship Bismarck: A Survivor's Story. Naval Institute Press, 1980.
sources.note: First-hand account by Bismarck's senior surviving officer.

sources.ref: Garzke, William H. and Dulin, Robert O. Battleships: Axis and Neutral Battleships in World War II. Naval Institute Press, 1985.
